name: Ye Olde Kings Head
address: 116 Santa Monica Blvd, Santa Monica, CA 90401
about: The oldest British pub in LA since 1974. The definitive home for England supporters on the Westside — expect a packed house, pints of Guinness, and commentary in English for every Three Lions match.

name: Escorpión
about: Upscale Mexican bar with a huge following among Argentine and Brazilian expats as well. Aguas frescas-cocktails, mezcal flights, and the loudest crowds in Mid-City for any CONMEBOL clash.

name: El Compadre
address: 7408 W Sunset Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90046
about: A Hollywood institution since 1975. Flaming margaritas, packed booths, and a loyal Mexican and Argentine fan base. Small screens but a massive atmosphere when El Tri or La Albiceleste play.

name: Red Lion Tavern
about: LA's best German beer hall, Silver Lake-style. Die Mannschaft supporters are loyal here — expect authentic brats, Oktoberfest steins, and commentary that switches to German for the big matches.

name: The Stadium Club
about: The beating heart of Koreatown sports culture. When the Korean national team plays, this place transforms — Korean commentary blasting, soju flowing, and a crowd that makes SoFi Stadium feel quiet.

name: Café Brasil
address: 11736 W Pico Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90064
about: A West LA institution for the Brazilian diaspora. Açaí bowls by day, green-and-gold war paint by night. One of the few spots where you'll hear commentary in Portuguese and find every Brasil match on.

name: Highland Park Bowl
address: 5621 N Figueroa St, Los Angeles, CA 90042
about: A beautifully restored 1920s bowling alley that doubles as one of NE LA's best bar experiences. Mixed crowd of Brazilian, Portuguese, and Argentine fans share lanes and big screens above the pins.

name: Casey's Irish Bar
address: 613 S Grand Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90017
about: Downtown's go-to Irish pub for over 30 years. Casey's pulls a big French and American crowd alongside the expat Irish regulars. Great location for catching matches before or after exploring DTLA.

name: Cock & Bull
about: The scrappy, unpretentious counterpart to Kings Head on the Westside. Smaller, cheaper, and just as passionate about England. Corner booths, proper pints, and regulars who've been coming since Italia 90.

name: LALA's Argentine Grill
about: The undisputed #1 spot for Argentine fans in LA. When La Albiceleste plays, LALA's is the closest thing to Buenos Aires you'll find west of the Mississippi. Reserve early — it sells out for every Argentina match.

name: Barney's Beanery
address: 8447 Santa Monica Blvd, West Hollywood, CA 90069
about: A Los Angeles landmark since 1920. Over 200 beers on tap, 70+ screens throughout the space, and the broadest mix of nationalities of any bar on this list. If your team isn't at a cultural bar, come here — someone's always watching.

name: Le Petit Paris
address: 418 S Spring St, Los Angeles, CA 90013
about: Authentic Parisian bistro in the heart of DTLA's Spring Street arts corridor. Les Bleus supporters dress up, the kitchen does match-day prix-fixe, and the zinc bar turns very loud when France score.

name: Big Wangs
about: Hollywood's loud, unapologetic sports bar. 30+ screens, wings by the bucket, and a crowd that spans the entire spectrum — USA diehards, Mexican supporters, and Brazilian regulars all coexist under one roof.

name: The Greyhound
address: 2659 Griffith Park Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90039
about: A proper Silver Lake local for England and Germany fans. Not flashy — wooden booths, real ales, and the kind of crowd that knows every player's name. Gets surprisingly electric for big England or Germany knockout matches.

name: The Brig
about: Venice's coolest dive bar, tucked on Abbot Kinney. A rotating cast of US, Brazilian, and Argentine regulars. Low-key for group stages but the energy builds fast in the knockouts.

name: The Association
about: A craft cocktail bar that plays unexpectedly well for the French and Portuguese crowds. Exposed brick, high ceilings, smart TVs — it's stylish but the football passion is completely genuine.

name: Mercado Buenos Aires
address: 7420 Van Nuys Blvd, Van Nuys, CA 91405
about: Deep in the San Fernando Valley, Mercado Buenos Aires is the authentic Argentine community hub. No tourists, all locals — the atmosphere on Argentina match nights is something you'll remember for years.

name: The Fox and Hounds
about: A proper English pub near UCLA with a loyal Westside following. Multiple screens, a good selection of British ales, and a civilised atmosphere even in the rowdiest knockout stages.

name: La Cita Bar
address: 336 S Hill St, Los Angeles, CA 90013
about: One of DTLA's most beloved Mexican bars. Colombian fans have adopted it too — the Latin energy is infectious, the drinks are stiff, and the screens are always on for any match featuring a CONMEBOL side.

name: The Short Stop
about: Echo Park's iconic late-night bar. A melting pot crowd that skews young, loud, and passionate. Reliable for USA matches and increasingly popular with English and Colombian fans who've discovered its cheap drinks and big TVs.

name: Tony's Darts Away
about: A craft-beer and darts pub in Burbank that has become the Valley's top English pub experience. Heavy England and USA crowd. All-vegan food menu, 38 California craft beers on tap, and early morning coverage of European kickoffs.

name: Bossa Nova
address: 685 N Robertson Blvd, West Hollywood, CA 90069
about: WeHo's beloved Brazilian restaurant turns into a samba-fuelled war room when A Seleção plays. Brazilian expats pack out the dining room, caipirinhas flow until last call, and the football is always on the big screen.

name: Ye Rustic Inn
about: Los Feliz's beloved dive bar since 1947. A fiercely loyal neighbourhood spot that comes alive for World Cup — USA and England fans claim the bar stools, Germany supporters take the back booths, and nobody leaves until extra time is over.

name: Far Bar
about: Tucked inside the Far East Plaza in Little Tokyo, Far Bar is the go-to spot for Japanese football fans in LA. An izakaya-meets-sports-bar vibe — yakitori, Japanese whisky, and every Samurai Blue match on the screens.

name: La Descarga
address: 1159 N Western Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90029
about: Hollywood's rum-and-cigar speakeasy turned World Cup den. Colombian and Mexican fans flood La Descarga for the combination of Caribbean energy and big-screen football. Book ahead — it fills up fast even on weeknights.

name: El Cholo
address: 1121 S Western Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90006
about: LA's oldest Mexican restaurant, open since 1923. A multi-generational gathering place for Mexico supporters — grandfathers, parents, and kids all come here to watch El Tri. The margaritas are legendary and the atmosphere is as warm as it gets.

name: Public School 213
address: 37 Washington Blvd, Marina Del Rey, CA 90292
about: A sleek gastropub on the Marina with serious sports bar credentials. Beachside crowd mixes USA and German supporters. High ceilings, craft IPAs, and a great patio for outdoor viewing when the weather holds.

name: Normandie Club
about: Koreatown's stylish cocktail lounge that fills with Korean-American supporters during Taeguk Warriors matches. Premium cocktails, DJ nights on weekends, and a slightly more sophisticated Koreatown football-watching experience than the dive bar alternatives.

name: The Other Room
about: A low-lit, narrow craft beer bar on Venice's main drag. Beloved by the Brazilian and Argentine beach crowd who make Abbot Kinney home. Intimate and passionate — feel every goal in this cosy space.

name: El Rancho Grande
about: The Valley's most passionate football bar for the Mexican and Colombian communities. When El Tri or Los Cafeteros play, every table is reserved days in advance. Mexican food, cold cervezas, and goal celebrations that shake the walls.

name: Lucky Baldwin's
about: Pasadena's legendary English pub, steps from the Rose Bowl. Lucky Baldwin's has been serving real ales and hosting football fans since 1996. When England or Germany play, every booth is claimed by dawn — and the outdoor patio erupts on every goal.

name: Goal Sports Cafe
address: 3200 Los Feliz Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90039
about: Atwater Village's dedicated supporter group hub. Goal Sports Cafe is where LAFC and USMNT fans gather — 8 screens, a massive beer list, and an electric atmosphere that's become the northeast LA headquarters for American Outlaws matchday watch parties.

name: Joxer Daly's
about: A proper Irish pub in the heart of Culver City. Joxer Daly's is intimate, authentic, and fiercely loyal to English and Irish football. Four screens, proper pints of Guinness, and a crowd that treats every England match like a cup final.

name: La Taqueria
about: The heartland of El Tri fandom in LA. La Taqueria in East LA transforms on Mexico match days — Spanish commentary at full blast, al pastor on the grill, and a rowdy crowd that spills out onto 1st Street. Colombian and Argentine fans are regulars too.

name: Busby's East
address: 3110 N Vermont Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90027
about: Named after Sir Matt Busby, this Los Feliz sports bar is purpose-built for matchday. Six big screens, 200 capacity, and a crowd that mixes USA, England, and German supporters in one of the most electric football atmospheres on the Eastside.

name: The Blue Room
address: 916 S San Fernando Blvd, Burbank, CA 91502
about: A no-frills Burbank dive bar with genuine character. The Blue Room is the kind of place where you pull up a stool, order a cheap beer, and watch the match in peace. USA and England fans have quietly made this their go-to on the east Valley side.

name: Sonny McLean's
about: Santa Monica's legendary Irish sports bar opens at 4am for early European kickoffs. Six screens, 150 capacity, and an electric mix of English, American, and Irish fans who've been coming here for decades. The Guinness is poured right and the atmosphere never disappoints.

name: Mercado de Paloma
address: 3655 S Grand Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90007
about: A vibrant food market near USC that sets up big screens for every major match. Mercado de Paloma is the rowdiest, most authentic Latin American football experience in DTLA — Mexican, Colombian, and Peruvian vendors blast commentary while the crowd roars between bites of ceviche and tamales.

name: Das Bootshaus
about: The only German bar on LA's Westside. Das Bootshaus brings proper Bavarian beer, brats, and Die Mannschaft passion to El Segundo. Small but mighty — when Germany plays, every seat is taken and the steins are raised before kickoff.

name: The Galley
about: The oldest bar in Santa Monica, pouring since 1934. The Galley is a chill, nautical-themed dive with three screens and a loyal crowd of England, USA, and Australian football fans. No frills, just good drinks and honest matchday vibes by the beach.

name: Bar Avalon
about: Koreatown's electric football lounge. Bar Avalon draws South Korean and Japanese fans with soju cocktails, K-BBQ platters, and five screens dedicated to Asian football. When the Taeguk Warriors or Samurai Blue play, this place is standing room only.

name: El Conquistador
address: 3701 W Sunset Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90026
about: A rowdy Los Feliz cantina where Mexican and Argentine fans take over on matchday. Three screens, cheap tequila shots, and an atmosphere that gets louder with every goal. El Conquistador is the neighbourhood's undisputed Latin football bar.

name: The Pikey
address: 7617 W Sunset Blvd, Los Angeles, CA 90046
about: A beloved Hollywood institution with deep British and Irish roots, The Pikey draws an eclectic mix of expats and football obsessives. Expect a heaving crowd for Ireland matches — and any England-Ireland fixture is best experienced nowhere else in the city.

name: Britannia Pub
about: A cornerstone of the British and Irish expat community in West Hollywood. Britannia is intimate, passionate, and packed to the rafters for any British Isles international. Irish and English fans share the space in good spirits — unless they are playing each other.

name: La Bodega Tapas Bar
about: Authentic Spanish tapas bar that transforms on World Cup matchdays when La Roja play. The Westside Spanish community rallies here — patatas bravas, jamón ibérico, and real passion for Spanish football from the tiki-taka era onwards.

name: Bar Pintxo
address: 109 Santa Monica Blvd, Santa Monica, CA 90401
about: Joe Miller s acclaimed Spanish pintxos bar and one of the best spots in LA for a proper European football atmosphere. Small, passionate, and deeply Spanish — La Roja fans have claimed this as their Westside match-day home.

name: Vermont Bar
address: 1714 N Vermont Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90027
about: A neighbourhood favourite in Los Feliz that doubles as an Irish and British expat hub for big football events. Low-key and deeply passionate — the Ireland faithful in the Eastside know this is their spot when the Boys in Green are on.

name: El Farolito
address: 4524 E César E Chávez Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90022
about: A true East LA institution. El Farolito transforms on El Tri matchdays into something between a watch party and a street celebration — the volume goes up, the aguas frescas come out, and the whole block feels it. This is where East LA cheers on Mexico with the intensity it deserves.

name: Rocco's Tavern
address: 4463 Van Nuys Blvd, Sherman Oaks, CA 91403
about: One of LA's most reliable sports bar chains, Rocco's Tavern in Sherman Oaks packs wall-to-wall screens and a crowd that shows up for every major match. A genuine multi-nationality hub — England fans, American Outlaws, and everyone in between finds a seat here. Great for early-round group stage watching when you want atmosphere without the hipster tax.

name: The Northern Standard
about: Silver Lake's gathering point for Canadian expats in the entertainment industry. Hockey pennants give way to Canada scarves on World Cup matchdays — bilingual English/French atmosphere, local craft beers, and a crowd who genuinely feel the magnitude of their co-host moment. Best place to back Les Rouges on the Eastside.

name: Block Heater
about: Named after the essential Canadian winter gadget, Block Heater is the go-to for LA's Canadian film and TV community during major sporting events. Co-host nation pride is taken seriously — expect a packed bar in red-and-white for every Canada match, with hockey regulars converting to football disciples for the summer.

name: The Dutch Colonial
about: Santa Monica's home for Dutch football passion. A small but fiercely vocal Dutch expat community makes this beachside venue a proper Oranje stronghold on matchday — orange kits, Dutch lagers, and tactical debate you'd expect from the nation that invented Total Football. The only bar in LA flying the Netherlands flag.

name: Casablanca Lounge
about: LA closest thing to a dedicated Moroccan football bar. Tea, tajine, and Atlas Lions pride on every screen. The Moroccan community in Culver City turns out in force for AFCON and World Cup.

name: El Condor (Sunset)
about: Echo Park go-to for Ecuadorian and wider South American football. Tri-Colores flags on the wall, Spanish commentary, and a crowd that gets louder with every Ecuador goal.

name: The Caledonian
address: 8615 Santa Monica Blvd, West Hollywood, CA 90069
about: The Tartan Army LA outpost. Scotland shirts on the wall, proper Scottish bitter on tap, and a crowd that shows up even when Scotland does not. The only bar in LA that goes quiet every time a goal goes against the Scots.

name: Atlas Sports Lounge
address: 655 S Hope St, Downtown, Los Angeles, CA 90017
about: Named for the Atlas Lions, this Downtown bar draws the North African diaspora from across Greater LA. Arabic and French commentary, Moroccan mint tea at half-time, wall-to-wall screens for Atlas Lions watches.

name: Village Idiot
about: A proper Melrose gastropub with serious sports credentials. Village Idiot has multiple screens, decent beer selection, and draws an eclectic mix of England, Ireland, USA, and Colombia fans. Open from noon every match day with dedicated viewing setups.

name: El Güero Canelo
address: 5720 N Figueroa St, Los Angeles, CA 90042
about: A beloved Highland Park cantina that transforms into an El Tri fortress on matchday. With its backyard patio, Spanish commentary on every screen, and a crowd that arrives in full Mexico kit, El Güero Canelo is where Highland Park celebrates Los Tri.

name: Crane Brewing DTLA
about: A craft beer taproom in the Arts District that doubles as an unlikely football haven. Crane Brewing has a large projection screen, communal tables, and a rotating crowd of USA, Netherlands, and Germany fans who appreciate both their hops and their tactics.

name: Maccheroni Republic
about: An Italian restaurant and bar in the heart of Downtown that goes full European on World Cup days. Expect passionate crowds watching Spain, France, and Portugal — great atmosphere, strong cocktail list, and the best pasta in the Arts District.

name: Tom Bergin's Tavern
address: 840 S Fairfax Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90036
about: One of LA’s oldest Irish bars, open since 1936. Tom Bergin’s is a proper pub time-warp — dark wood, Guinness on tap, shamrocks on the ceiling. Ireland and England fans have been gathering here for decades. Expect full mid-city energy for the knockout rounds.

name: El Condor
about: Echo Park’s favourite Mexican and Colombian bar. El Condor is standing-room only when El Tri or La Tricolor plays — Spanish commentary, outdoor patio, and a vibe that feels more Guadalajara than LA. Closed Mondays.

name: La Cuevita
address: 5922 N Figueroa St, Los Angeles, CA 90042
about: Highland Park’s best mezcal bar doubles as the neighbourhood’s El Tri headquarters. La Cuevita has low lighting, killer agave spirits, and a loyal crew of Mexico, Colombia, and USA fans who fill the place for every big match.

name: La Barca Sports Bar
about: The closest dedicated sports bar to SoFi Stadium. La Barca is where Inglewood locals and Mexico fans gather — a 10-minute walk from the stadium gates, with Spanish commentary, outdoor screens, and a crowd that starts loud and gets louder. The go-to pre/post-SoFi spot.

name: Rush Street
about: Culver City’s main sports bar event. Rush Street runs 30+ screens, a rooftop deck, and knows how to pack a crowd. USA, England, and Germany fans dominate the regulars — with strong Colombia and Argentina representation from the surrounding creative industry crowd.

name: Brennan's Pub
address: 4089 Lincoln Blvd, Marina del Rey, CA 90292
about: Marina del Rey’s beloved Irish pub — a proper neighbourhood local since 1959. Brennan’s is intimate, friendly, and absolutely packed when Ireland or England plays. The turtle racing tradition is on hold for the tournament, replaced by football on every screen.

name: The Federal Bar
about: The best sports bar in the Valley. Federal Bar has an enormous screen wall, standing room of 400+, and a crowd that spans every nationality in NoHo. USA and England fans fill the front; Argentina and Mexico contingents take over the back room for the big matches.

name: Hermosillo
about: Highland Park’s coolest cocktail bar goes full El Tri mode for World Cup 2026. Hermosillo draws a young Mexican and Colombian crowd from across Northeast LA — agave-forward drinks, Spanish commentary, and a packed patio when Mexico plays.

name: Golden Road Brewing
address: 5410 W San Fernando Rd, Los Angeles, CA 90039
about: LA’s most popular brewery has a massive beer garden that becomes the city’s best outdoor World Cup venue. Golden Road runs giant projection screens, serves 30+ beers on tap, and holds 500+ people — ideal for big group stage and knockout matches.

name: Bar Lubitsch
address: 7702 Santa Monica Blvd, West Hollywood, CA 90046
about: WeHo’s Eastern European-inspired bar is a hidden gem for European football fans. Bar Lubitsch draws German, Austrian, and French supporters — vodkas, schnapps, and a crowd that actually cares about the group stage. Quietly one of the best spots in the city for continental European football.

name: Alex's Bar
address: 2913 E Anaheim St, Long Beach, CA 90804
about: Long Beach’s best dive bar hosts the city’s loudest World Cup watch parties. Alex’s Bar is a no-frills, maximum-atmosphere spot drawing Mexico and USA fans from the Long Beach Latino community — cheap beers, loud crowds, and pure football culture.

name: La Poubelle
about: Hollywood’s favourite French bistro-bar packs out for France and Portugal matches. La Poubelle has been a Francophile institution since 1972 — intimate, noisy, and brilliant when les Bleus play. Book a table; walk-ins are optimistic for France knockout games.

name: Silverlake Lounge
about: Silver Lake’s indie bar scene meets football culture at this classic Sunset Strip dive. Silverlake Lounge draws a creative, international crowd — USA and Mexico fans with a healthy contingent of Brazilian and Colombian regulars. Low-key for group stage, properly rowdy when the stakes rise.

name: Dan Sung Sa
about: The most iconic Korean beer hall in America. Dan Sung Sa is a legendary hof — an old-school Korean drinking den where beer towers flow alongside plates of dried squid and fried chicken until 4am. When South Korea play, this place transforms: flags on every wall, screaming on every goal, and an energy that can't be manufactured. This is where LA's Korean community has watched football for decades, and it's the loudest room in the city when the Taeguk Warriors score.

name: The Prince
about: One of LA’s most storied bars, The Prince has been a Hollywood haunt since 1949 and appeared in Mad Men, New Girl, and countless films. Dark wood panelling, red leather booths, and cocktails that don't mess around. Tucked into the heart of Koreatown, it draws a fascinating mix of cinephiles, Korean-Americans, and anyone who appreciates an institution. For World Cup matches, it’s an unlikely gem — close to the Korean community, reliably packed, and with an atmosphere that money can’t buy.

name: Musha
about: Santa Monica’s beloved Japanese izakaya has been a Westside institution since 1997. Musha draws LA’s Japanese community to its corner of Main Street for yakitori, cold Sapporo, and late-night camaraderie that feels transplanted from Tokyo. When the Samurai Blue play, Musha becomes the go-to for Japanese fans on the Westside — Japanese commentary on the screens, the right food on the table, and a crowd who knows every player’s number. One of the most genuinely atmospheric Japan matches in the city.

name: Yakitoriya
about: In the heart of Sawtelle Japantown — LA’s Little Osaka — Yakitoriya is where serious Japanese football fans gather. The authentic yakitori bar has been screening Japan matches for years, drawing Japanese expats and Japanese-Americans who want their football with sake and skewers rather than nachos and bud light. Small, intimate, and passionately Japanese. For Samurai Blue matches, book ahead or arrive early — this place fills up fast and the energy is worth every minute.

name: Yard House
about: Less than 10 miles from the Rose Bowl, Yard House Pasadena is one of the most important watch venues in this guide. With 35 screens covering every wall and a 130-beer draft list, it was built for exactly this kind of tournament. Capacity for 300+ fans, sight lines from every seat, and the kind of professional setup that handles a packed World Cup crowd without missing a beat. For USA matches especially, this will be at full capacity — book a table or arrive well before kickoff.

name: Naja's Place
about: A South Bay institution that has been showing World Cups since 1982. Naja's Place sits on the Redondo Beach Pier with 90+ beers on tap, 10 screens, and a loyal football crowd that drives in from Manhattan Beach, Torrance, and Hawthorne for the big matches. England fans, USA supporters, and football purists who want atmosphere without the Hollywood circus come here. There’s an outdoor section facing the water that makes late-night celebrations particularly spectacular. One of LA’s most authentic football pubs, and criminally underrated.

name: El Tepeyac Cafe
address: 812 N Evergreen Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90033
about: Boyle Heights legend since 1952. Manuel's Burrito and a packed house when El Tri plays. The real East LA watch party spot — everyone knows this place.

name: La Serenata de Garibaldi
about: Beloved Boyle Heights seafood restaurant that transforms on match days. Outdoor screens, cold cervezas, and the whole neighbourhood cheering El Tri together.

name: El Gallo Bar
address: 4328 E Cesar E Chavez Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90022
about: Classic East LA cantina where Cesar Chavez Ave gets loud on match days. Central American nations well represented — Mexico, El Salvador, Guatemala all get the same electric treatment.

name: Eastside Luv Wine Bar
about: Boyle Heights most culturally rich bar — part wine bar, part neighbourhood hangout. Outdoor patio, regular football events, and a crowd passionate about the beautiful game.

name: Raffi's Place
about: The heartbeat of Glendale's Armenian community. Raffi's is where the diaspora gathers for big matches — the French and European games draw massive Armenian crowds. Legendary kebab.

name: Carousel Restaurant
about: Glendale's most iconic Armenian restaurant, famous for mezze and manti. On World Cup nights the dining room transforms. The Armenian community comes out in force for France and Belgium — two countries with huge Armenian populations.

name: Public House Glendale
about: Glendale's go-to sports bar with 14 screens and outdoor patio seating. Mixed crowd but a strong Armenian contingent, especially for European matches. Reliably shows every game.

name: Porto's Bakery & Cafe
about: LA's legendary Cuban bakery opens its sprawling Glendale space for big match days. Extended hours, outdoor screens, pastelitos, and a festive Latin atmosphere. Unique watch party spot.
